content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Richard E. Archibald 
Deputy city Attorney 
City of Sacramento 
812 Tenth Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Archibald: 
July 12, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-259 
We have received your letter concerning the new 
restrictions on publicly-funded mass mailings under Proposition 
73. 
Enclosed is a copy of follow-up advice to Vance Raye and 
other state and local agency representatives interpreting the 
mass mailing restrictions. 
This letter and the June 16, 1988 
letter are intended as guidance to all persons similarly 
situated, and may therefore be relied upon until the Commission 
takes further action. 
In regard to your specific question concerning notices of 
"town hall meetings" sent at public expense, please refer to 
paragraph 3 on page 4 of the July 1, 1988 letter. 
Based on 
that paragraph, your Exhibit "2" would be permitted because it 
contains no reference to any elected official. 
However, your 
Exhibit "3" could not be prepared and sent at public expense 
because it bears the signature of an elected official. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Richard E. Archibald 
July 12, 1988 
Page 2 
In the future, we expect t,o address additional questions 
concerning the mass mailing restrictions in proposition 73, and 
we will include your name on our mailing list for information 
on the subject. 
DMG:KED:ld 
Enclosure 
Sincerel~l , 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
~~'L~ 
f~,1:rv--v~l-tLv __ 
By: 
Kath~yn E. Donovan 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question_synthetic: Whether town hall meeting notices sent at public expense must comply with Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions, specifically whether the presence of an elected official's signature affects permissibility.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Exhibit 2 (town hall notice with no reference to elected official) may be sent at public expense, but Exhibit 3 (bearing an elected official's signature) cannot be prepared and sent at public expense under Proposition 73 restrictions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document structured as advice letter response rather than traditional Q/A format. Question and conclusion are inferred from body text rather than explicitly labeled sections. References prior FPPC letter dated July 1, 1988 for detailed guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether town hall meeting notices sent at public expense must comply with Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions, specifically whether the presence of an elected official's signature affects permissibility.

CONCLUSION: Exhibit 2 (town hall notice with no reference to elected official) may be sent at public expense, but Exhibit 3 (bearing an elected official's signature) cannot be prepared and sent at public expense under Proposition 73 restrictions.

This letter and the June 16, 1988 letter are intended as guidance to all persons similarly situated, and may therefore be relied upon until the Commission takes further action. embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the City of Sacramento's inquiry about whether town hall meeting notices sent at public expense comply with Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions. The FPPC advises that notices without elected official references are permitted, but those bearing an elected official's signature are prohibited.
